text: We thank the SAGES selection committee for the opportunity to present this compilation of bariatric surgery revisions with use of the robotic platform. We have no disclosures. Revisional bariatrics represent an increasing proportion of bariatric caseload, now accounting for up to twenty percent of the total case volume. Indications include weight regain, marginal ulcer, and refractory reflux, among others. These procedures are technically challenging with morbidity ranging from eight to forty-six percent. Trocar placement is typically as shown. We used two eight millimeter and two twelve millimeter ports, as well as a Nathanson retractor.

The surgeon begins by dissecting the crura using electrocautery and blunt dissection, creating a retroesophageal window and placing a Penrose drain for esophageal retraction. The robotic platform provides clear visualization of the mediastinum for hernia sac reduction. After confirming at least two centimeters of intra-abdominal esophageal length, a posterior choroplasty is performed using non-absorbable zero-barb suture. An upper endoscopy is conducted to assess the pouch, followed by placation under direct endoscopic view to address dilation. The case concludes after satisfactory evaluation of the pouch diameter and GJ anastomosis appearance.

The surgeon performs lysis of adhesions between the gastric pouch and retained fundus, noting a staple line extending onto the diaphragm. The retained fundus is mobilized and resected using a linear robotic stapler with a green load, followed by a second blue load fire with buttressing material. Upper endoscopy confirms an appropriately sized pouch with no remaining fundus, and the specimen is removed from the abdomen.

The surgeon performs lysis of adhesions to define the anatomy, noting dense adherence of the gastric pouch to the GJ anastomosis and gastric remnant. Multiple clips from the prior procedure are visible. A posterior window to the Roux limb is developed, revealing a long, adherent redundant portion. A retro gastric window is created behind the pouch, and a Penrose drain is placed for retraction. The Roux limb is divided distal to the anastomosis using a stapler. The jejunum is brought up to the pouch for a tension-free revised gastrojejunal anastomosis. A mesenteric window in the redundant candy cane mesentery is divided with a vessel sealing device. Fluorescent angiography confirms robust perfusion to the gastric pouch. A XXXIV French bougie is placed, and a posterior gastrotomy is created. A blue load robotic stapler is used for the posterior anastomosis, and the bougie is advanced into the small bowel. The anterior portion is closed with zero running V-lock absorbable suture, buttressed with interrupted two O silks. The candy cane limb is resected with a white load stapler, and the specimen is removed. The anastomosis is reinforced with surgical glue, and a drain is placed. Conclusion EGD shows a hemostatic anastomosis.

In conclusion, revisional bariatric procedures can be very technically challenging. Early definition of the anatomy is critical. The robotic platform is ideally suited to these procedures due to superior three-D visualization, improved dexterity and precision, and reduction of torque from the thick abdominal wall. Thank you for your time.
